questions.prompt: What is "click approve and see" in the lesson's framing?
choices: Taking an action when you do not fully understand what it does, on the assumption you will find out as it executes.

choices.label: Approving a recommendation after carefully reading and understanding it.
choices.rationale: That is the correct disposition. The lesson's reflex is the opposite.

choices.label: Asking the SOC a clarifying question before acting.
choices.rationale: The opposite reflex, the one the lesson installs.

choices.label: Bulk-closing similar-looking incidents to clear the queue.
choices.rationale: That is Trap 1 from the helpdesk-traps lesson, a related but different failure mode.

questions.explanation: Action without understanding, justified by 'we'll see.' The common shape across all the examples the lesson names.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the three reasons the lesson gives for why click-and-see ends careers?

choices.label: The platform's actions affect real customers.
choices.rationale: One of the three reasons named in the lesson.

choices.label: The action is recorded in the portal's audit log.
choices.rationale: One of the three reasons named in the lesson.

choices: Huntress automatically flags click-and-see patterns to your MSP's management.

choices.label: The pattern compounds and the tech's reliability score drops.
choices.rationale: One of the three reasons named in the lesson.

questions.explanation: The lesson does not claim Huntress flags patterns to management. The senior reviewing your work catches the pattern; it is an internal MSP signal.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson call the portal "forgiving in the UI" a trap?
choices: Low UI friction (most actions just need a confirm dialog) does not match the action's real consequence. The cost is hidden until later, when the customer feels it.

choices.label: The UI has bugs that let you click the wrong button.
choices.rationale: The UI works correctly; that is part of the problem.

choices.label: The UI is hard to navigate so you will click something by accident.
choices.rationale: The lesson's point is the opposite. Actions are easy to take, which makes them dangerous.

choices.label: Huntress hides destructive actions behind hidden menus.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The actions are exposed and the dialog is the only friction.

questions.explanation: UI friction does not track action weight on a security platform. Low friction masks high consequence.

questions.prompt: What is the ten-second pause the lesson installs as a habit?

choices.label: Always wait five minutes before any portal action.
choices.rationale: Wrong duration and wrong criterion. The pause is brief and conditional.

choices: Ask yourself: do I know what this action does, on this customer, in this situation? If yes, click. If no, escalate or clarify.

choices.label: Ask Huntress support before any approval.
choices.rationale: Misuses support. Most approvals are routine; the pause is for the unsure ones.

choices.label: Refresh the portal twice before clicking.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing.

questions.explanation: The pause is the practice. Recognising the reflex matters more than memorising any specific rule.

questions.prompt: "The senior would be annoyed if I asked." What is wrong with this framing?

choices.label: Seniors are required to be friendly; they cannot be annoyed.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts and not the lesson's framing.

choices.label: Only ask via the ticket comment thread, never face-to-face.
choices.rationale: Wrong channel rule. The lesson allows senior, SOC reply, or runbook re-read.

choices: Almost never true. The senior is far more annoyed by recovery work from a wrong click than by a clarifying question. Asking is cheap; recovery is not.

choices.label: You should never ask the senior and always figure it out yourself.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson entirely.

questions.explanation: The senior's actual preference inverts the tech's assumption. Recovery work from a wrong click costs more than a thirty-second question.

questions.prompt: A new tech says: "Speed is the goal. Slow-and-correct will get me flagged for being too slow." What is the right correction?
choices: Speed at the cost of correctness on a security platform is the failure mode. Slow-and-correct beats fast-and-wrong every time.

choices.label: Speed is the goal, but only on Lows. Highs and Criticals deserve more care.
choices.rationale: Wrong calibration. All severity classes need the do-I-understand-this check.

choices.label: Tell the senior the queue is too big and refuse to work until it shrinks.
choices.rationale: Does not address the per-ticket correctness issue.

choices.label: Click approve faster on tickets that look similar to ones you have closed before.
choices.rationale: The autopilot pattern combined with click-and-see. Worse, not better.

questions.explanation: Queue pressure is real, but correctness is the metric the customer feels. The mismatch is what the reflex exploits.

questions.prompt: A peer clicked-and-seen on three tickets in a fortnight. The senior catches the pattern in review. Why does this matter more than any one bad click?

choices.label: Because Huntress raises the customer's subscription fee if click-and-see patterns are detected.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model. Click-and-see is not a billing event.

choices: A single bad click is recoverable. The pattern is not. Once flagged, the tech's reliability drops, the senior's trust drops, and the runway shortens.

choices.label: Each individual click is irreversible by design.
choices.rationale: Most portal actions are reversible at some cost. The lesson's point is the pattern, not individual reversibility.

choices.label: The pattern triggers an automatic Huntress support ticket against the tech.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Huntress does not auto-flag tech-side patterns.

questions.explanation: The compounding is the third reason careers end. A single miss is a learning moment; a pattern of misses is a reliability signal.

questions.prompt: Order the steps of the calibrated alternative to click-and-see when you are not confident about an action.
items: Notice the urge to click and pause for ten seconds.
Ask: do I know what this action does, on this customer, in this situation?
Name the uncertainty (to yourself, to the senior, or in a reply to the SOC).
Re-read the runbook, ask the senior, or reply to the SOC with the specific question.
Once resolved, proceed with the action or hand off cleanly.
questions.explanation: Pause, test, name, clarify, proceed. The recognition moment is the whole skill; without the pause, the rest does not happen.

questions.prompt: High-severity EDR Incident Report on WS-EXAMPLE-LEGAL-08. Recommendation: approve the autoruns remediation on C:\Program Files\LegalSoft\update.exe. You have never heard of LegalSoft. The customer is a law firm. What do you do?

choices.label: Click approve. The SOC said so.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation says approve, but the path looks like legitimate business-software. 'The SOC said so' is sometimes right and sometimes the cardinal mistake.

choices.label: Click approve to see if it breaks the customer's legal software.
choices.rationale: The verbatim reflex this lesson exists to prevent.

choices: Pause and reply to the SOC: the path matches an installed legal-industry software vendor for this customer. Confirm the binary classification is malicious despite the legitimate-vendor path, before approving.

choices.label: Close the incident as benign because the path looks like legitimate business software.
choices.rationale: Worse than click-and-see. Overriding the SOC's High-severity Recommendation on path alone is the cardinal mistake.

questions.explanation: Twenty seconds to write the question. One minute to wait. The analyst confirms (binary tampered, malicious despite the path) and you proceed with confidence, or clarifies and you avoid a customer-impact mistake.

questions.prompt: Continuing the LegalSoft scenario: the SOC analyst confirms the binary is malicious, the attacker dropped a fake update.exe into the LegalSoft directory. What do you do next?
choices: Approve the remediation and acknowledge the analyst with a one-line reply.

choices.label: Ask the analyst again, to be sure.
choices.rationale: Relitigating a clear answer. The analyst's response resolved the question.

choices.label: Click approve immediately without responding to the analyst.
choices.rationale: Functionally right, but skipping the acknowledgement is a courtesy gap.

choices.label: Also block the LegalSoft directory at the firewall to prevent re-drop.
choices.rationale: Extending the action set on context the SOC did not recommend. If you think the firewall should also act, that is another clarifying reply, not a unilateral extension.

questions.explanation: The pause-and-clarify did its job. The acknowledgement closes the loop cleanly with the SOC.
